chapter_context.summary: Pronounces the burden of Damascus and Ephraim, prophesying their ruin and thinness, yet promising a gleaning of fruit for those who look to their Maker.

outline.text: The Ruin of Damascus and Thinness of Jacob's Glory
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Ruin of Damascus and Thinness of Jacob's Glory (17:1-5), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Remnant Looking to their Maker; Sorrow of Idle Harvests
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Remnant Looking to their Maker; Sorrow of Idle Harvests (17:6-11), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Rebuke of Rushing Nations like Chaff Before the Wind
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Rebuke of Rushing Nations like Chaff Before the Wind (17:12-14), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

key_themes.title: Prophecy of Judgment
key_themes.description: This chapter explores key facets of prophecy of judgment, revealing how God's holy character directs both judgment and redemption.

key_themes.title: The Holy One of Israel
key_themes.description: Isaiah's signature title for God, highlighting His absolute transcendent holiness, moral purity, and unique relationship with His people.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the warnings and promises of Chapter 17, how does God's double character of absolute holiness and deep comfort speak to your life today?
How does this chapter encourage you to trust in God's promises of ultimate renewal and restoration in times of spiritual or physical exile?

cultural_historical.title: The Role of the Prophet in Israel
cultural_historical.text: Prophets in the Hebrew Bible were not just fortune-tellers, but 'covenant enforcement agents'—called to confront sin, demand social justice, and bring hope of future restoration.
